dataElements.name: drought-assessment-data-collection-point-geo-codes
dataElements.shortName: drought-assessment-data-collection-point-geo-codes
dataElements.description: Please collect GPS coordinates at the point of data collection. Ensure that the device's location services are enabled, and that you are physically present at the collection site before submitting.

dataElements.name: drought-hw-hr-1.1.1 Is the Health Workforce participating in drought, water and climate change adaptation plan and policies?*
dataElements.shortName: drought-hw-hr-1.1.1 Is the Health Workforce partic
dataElements.description: 1: No plan:There are no initiatives or involvement of the health workforce in adaptation plans or policies. No knowledge of ongoing planning or climate adaptation actions exists. 2: Minimal plan:There have been some internal discussions on involving staff in climate adaptation planning, but no structured approach has been defined. Staff participation is currently limited or informal. 3: Basic plan:A draft plan exists to involve health workers in adaptation policies, but it has not been fully implemented or integrated into routine operations. Staff engagement remains low or irregular. 4: Moderate plan:A formal plan is in place, and staff participate in climate-related adaptation activities, but the plan has not been recently reviewed to reflect new challenges or lessons learned. Participation is functional but may lack continuity. 5: Fully developed plan:The facility has a comprehensive, regularly updated plan that actively involves health workers in drought and climate adaptation strategies. Staff roles are clearly defined, and engagement is integrated into facility-wide operations.

dataElements.name:
drought-hw-hr-1.1.2 Is the Health Workforce equipped with a plan to identify minimum needs for health workers to ensure operational 
 sufficiency care services?
dataElements.shortName: drought-hw-hr-1.1.2 Is the Health Workforce equipp
dataElements.description: 1: No Plan in Place:No defined strategy to maintain staffing during drought conditions. 2: Minimal Plan in place:Some preliminary discussions have taken place to address health worker sufficiency, but no formal or documented plan exists. 3: Basic Plan Drafted:A draft plan outlines the minimum staffing needs during disruptions, but implementation has not been consistent or tested. The plan exists but lacks full integration into emergency response systems. 4: Moderate Plan in Place, Not Updated:A functional plan is in place and considers staffing needs during droughts; however, it may not reflect recent changes in workforce or service demand. Updates or drills are limited. 5: Fully Updated and Operational Plan:The facility has an updated, operational plan that ensures minimum staffing levels are maintained even during prolonged droughts. Staffing adjustments are proactively managed through this plan.

dataElements.name:
drought-hw-hr-1.1.3 Is the Health Workforce equipped with a plan for scheduling outdoor work for cooler time of the day and reducing 
 physical demand during hot days?
dataElements.shortName: drought-hw-hr-1.1.3 Is the Health Workforce equipp
dataElements.description: 1: No Plan in Place:No guidance for modifying work hours based on heat stress. 2: Minimal Plan with Initial Discussions:Consideration underway but no formal plan has been documented or communicated to staff. 3: Basic Plan Drafted:Draft policy in place but untested. 4: Moderate Plan in Place, Not Updated:Work schedule plan is outdated or not widely applied. 5: Fully Updated and Operational Plan:A clear and enforced policy exists to adapt outdoor work conditions based on heat and time of day.

dataElements.name:
drought-hw-hr-1.1.4 Is the Health Workforce provided with sunscreen, hat and plenty of drinking water for staff carrying out outdoor 
 activities?
dataElements.shortName: drought-hw-hr-1.1.4 Is the Health Workforce provid
dataElements.description: 1: No Provision in place:No provisions are made for outdoor protection. 2: Minimal provision:Acknowledgement of need, but no supply system in place. 3: Basic provision:A policy draft exists with procurement steps outlined. 4: Moderate provision:Some provisions are available but not consistent. 5: Full provision:Staff receive essential protective supplies regularly with monitoring in place.

dataElements.name: drought-hw-hr-1.1.5 Is the Health Workforce provided with drinking water and stimulated regularly for appropriate water intake?
dataElements.shortName: drought-hw-hr-1.1.5 Is the Health Workforce provid
dataElements.description: 1: No Plan in place to ensure adequate provision of safe water:There is no system for ensuring staff access to drinking water or encouraging hydration. 2: Minimal plan with Initial Discussions:Discussion ongoing but no actionable steps taken. 3: Basic Plan Drafted:A policy draft exists to promote hydration but not yet implemented. 4: Moderate Plan in Place, Not Updated:Hydration support is partially in place but not systematic. Some departments may apply it more rigorously than others. 5: Fully Updated and Operational Plan:A robust hydration strategy with regular reminders and water provision is active and monitored.

dataElements.name: drought-hw-cd-1.2.1 Is the Health Workforce trained to identify health conditions made worse by drought?
dataElements.shortName: drought-hw-cd-1.2.1 Is the Health Workforce traine
dataElements.description: 1: No Training:There is no training provided to help health workers recognise health conditions that may be exacerbated by drought. As a result, staff are ill-equipped to detect or respond appropriately to drought-related health issues.. 2: Minimal Training:Some awareness exists about the impact of drought on health, but no formal or consistent training has been provided. Knowledge is limited to individual experiences or external sources. 3: Basic Training:Some staff have received basic formal training on public health and climate change, with limited but relevant content on heatwave-related health impacts. Training is not standardized or widely implemented. 4: Moderate Training:Staff were trained previously, but content is outdated. 5: Full/Comprehensive Training:Staff are regularly trained on identifying and managing drought-related illnesses.

dataElements.name:
drought-hw-cd-1.2.2 equipped with knowledge, experience, training and resources to manage emergency 
 preparedness plans and response measures to reduce drought risks and impacts at the 
 facility and in the local communities?*
dataElements.shortName: drought-hw-cd-1.2.2 equipped with knowledge, exper
dataElements.description: 1: No Training:There is no structured preparedness training or resources for managing drought-related emergencies in the facility or community. Staff lack guidance and tools to respond effectively. 2: Minimal Training:Emergency preparedness has been discussed but lacks structure. 3: Basic Training:A draft plan and partial training exist but not system-wide. 4: Moderate Training:A preparedness plan exists and has been used but needs revision. 5: Full/Comprehensive Contingency Plan:Staff are equipped and regularly trained to reduce drought impacts within the facility and community.

dataElements.name: drought-hw-cd-1.2.3 Is the Health Workforce trained in multihazard assessments?
dataElements.shortName: drought-hw-cd-1.2.3 Is the Health Workforce traine
dataElements.description: 1: No Training in Place:No training on multihazard assessments. 2: Minimal Training Plan with Initial Discussions:The importance of multi-hazard assessment is acknowledged, but no structured training has been implemented. Interest exists, but execution is lacking. 3: Basic tranining plan:A draft training module on multi-hazard assessments is available but has not yet been broadly disseminated or tested in practice. Only select individuals may have been exposed to the content. 4: Moderate Plan in Place, Not Updated:Training on multi-hazard assessment has been rolled out to most health workers, though it may lack recent scenario updates or refresher courses. It serves as a useful foundation but needs more dynamic application. 5: Fully Updated tranining:Staff routinely trained on multihazard risk assessment frameworks and methodologies.

dataElements.name: drought-hw-cd-1.2.4 Is the Health Workforce trained to manage hazardous chemicals?
dataElements.shortName: drought-hw-cd-1.2.4 Is the Health Workforce traine
dataElements.description: 1: No training in Place:There is no training on the handling or disposal of hazardous chemicals, increasing risk to staff, patients, and the environment. 2: Minimal training Plan:Training concept discussed but not rolled out. 3: Basic Training Plan Drafted:Hazardous chemical training manual prepared but unimplemented. 4: Moderate Training Plan in Place, Not Updated:Some staff trained but programme not recently reviewed. 5: Fully Updated taining:Active training programme in place and regularly refreshed.

dataElements.name: drought-hw-cd-1.2.5 Is the Health Workforce trained on how to treat stored water for human consumption?
dataElements.shortName: drought-hw-cd-1.2.5 Is the Health Workforce traine
dataElements.description: 1: No Training/Guidance:No capacity on water treatment. 2: Minimal Training/Guidance:Plans in place but not executed. 3: Basic Training/Guidance:Water treatment training module drafted but not operational. 4: Moderate Training/Guidance in use:Water safety training has occurred but needs review. 5: Full Training/Guidance:Staff routinely trained and equipped with resources to ensure potable water safety.

dataElements.name:
drought-hw-cd-1.2.6 Is the Health Workforce trained or prepared to quantify drought-sensitive diseases taking into account the special 
 drought patterns?
dataElements.shortName: drought-hw-cd-1.2.6 Is the Health Workforce traine
dataElements.description: 1: No training or preparation in Place:No knowledge or tools to monitor drought-related diseases. 2: Minimal training/Prepation with Initial Discussions:Preliminary discussions ongoing. 3: Basic training and parataion plan Drafted:Draft tools or methods created but not yet deployed. 4: Moderate taining in Place, Not Updated:Some monitoring is occurring, but tools or training are not up-to-date. 5: Fully Updated training Plan:Monitoring systems and staff training are well-established for drought-sensitive health trends.

dataElements.name:
drought-hw-cd-1.2.7 Is the Health Workforce able to convey protective strategies for public health emergencies, in case of high 
 temperature effects, and water and food contamination to patients, staff and communities?*
dataElements.shortName: drought-hw-cd-1.2.7 Is the Health Workforce able t
dataElements.description: 1: No strategies in Place:Staff are not trained in risk communication. 2: Minimal strategies with Initial Discussions:Interest in developing communication strategies exists. 3: Basic strategies Drafted:Communication plan drafted but no training provided. 4: Moderate strategies in Place, Not Updated:Risk communication strategy exists but hasn’t been recently reviewed. 5: Fully Updated and Operational strategies:Staff are trained and actively engage in community and patient education on emergency protective strategies.

dataElements.name: drought-hw-cd-1.2.8 Is the Health Workforce trained to an appropriate standard to maintain the correct level of safety of electrical power supply, in both routine and emergency/disaster situations?*
dataElements.shortName: drought-hw-cd-1.2.8 Is the Health Workforce traine
dataElements.description: 1: No training in Place:There is no training or standard in place to help staff maintain electrical power systems safely. This leaves the facility vulnerable during outages or overload situations. 2: Minimal training:Discussions around training have started but but no training has been conducted. 3: Basic taining plan drafted:Training curriculum or SOPs created but not implemented. 4: Moderate training plan:Staff receive basic training to maintain power systems during emergencies, but procedures are not regularly updated. 5: Fully Updated trainig:Staff are trained and certified to manage electrical safety during both routine and emergency operations. Procedures are routinely rehearsed and reviewed for improvements.

dataElements.name: drought-hw-car-1.3.1 Is the Health Workforce aware of the different impacts of drought on human health?
dataElements.shortName: drought-hw-car-1.3.1 Is the Health Workforce aware
dataElements.description: 1: No Awareness:There is no structured education or awareness campaign on how drought affects health. 2: Minimal Awareness:Awareness about drought impacts has been discussed informally or during staff meetings. However, no formal education sessions or materials have been disseminated. 3: Basic Awareness:There is a draft awareness plan or guidance document outlining drought-related health issues. Some staff have been introduced to this content, but coverage is inconsistent. 4: Moderate Awareness:Most staff are aware of key drought-related health impacts due to prior training sessions or workshops, although the content has not been recently reviewed or expanded. 5: Full Awareness:All relevant staff have received updated and ongoing training on the multiple health impacts of drought, including chronic conditions, mental health, and infectious diseases.

dataElements.name: drought-hw-car-1.3.2 informed of air pollution advisories and warnings?
dataElements.shortName: drought-hw-car-1.3.2 informed of air pollution adv
dataElements.description: 1: No information/Awareness:There is no system to inform health workers about air quality issues during droughts. Staff are unaware of potential respiratory risks linked to dust and dry conditions. 2: Minimal information/Awareness:Some discussions on air pollution impacts have occurred, but there's no formal alert system. Health workers rely on external news sources for updates. 3: Basic information/Awareness:A basic plan to receive and relay air quality alerts exists but is not yet fully implemented. A few individuals have access to updates, but there's no formal dissemination process. 4: Moderate information/Awareness:An air quality monitoring and alert system is partially functional, and staff receive occasional updates. However, integration into routine operations is still lacking. 5: Full information/Awareness:The facility receives real-time air pollution advisories, and staff are routinely informed and trained to act accordingly. Warnings are clearly communicated through internal systems and action plans are activated promptly.

dataElements.name: drought-hw-car-1.3.3 Is the Health Workforce prepared with clear messaging about water and food safety during and after a drought?
dataElements.shortName: drought-hw-car-1.3.3 Is the Health Workforce prepa
dataElements.description: 1: No messaing:No preparedness or messaging exists concerning water and food safety in drought conditions. The community and staff may unknowingly consume unsafe supplies. 2: Minimal Awareness:Basic awareness exists, but staff have not received formal training. Knowledge is superficial, and staff are unable to effectively use or interpret the system. 3: Basic Awareness:Some staff have received introductory training and can perform limited functions within the system. Use is irregular, and there may be uncertainty around interpreting the data or taking appropriate follow-up actions. 4: Moderate Awareness:Most relevant staff are adequately trained and informed. They can competently use the surveillance system to enter, monitor, and report health data, with moderate guidance. 5: Full Awareness:All designated staff are thoroughly trained and fully informed on how to operate and follow the surveillance system. They can accurately track, analyze, and respond to health outcomes independently, and the system is well-integrated into routine public health monitoring and decision-making.

dataElements.name: drought-hw-car-1.3.4 informed on how to use and follow a surveillance system to track health outcomes?
dataElements.shortName: drought-hw-car-1.3.4 informed on how to use and fo
dataElements.description: 1: No awareness/information There is no training or system in place for staff to monitor health indicators during droughts. Health outcomes linked to drought go untracked.: 2: Minimal Awareness/information:Discussions on the use of health surveillance systems have started, but staff are not yet trained or supported in implementation. Monitoring is informal or inconsistent. 3: Basic Awareness/information:A draft plan and toolset for surveillance exists, and limited training has been offered. Some staff have practiced data entry or reporting. 4: Moderate Awareness/information:Staff use a surveillance system to monitor drought-related health outcomes, although the system may not be fully integrated or updated. Reporting is done regularly but could be improved. 5: Full Awareness/information:Health workers are well-trained in using surveillance systems to track and report drought-sensitive health indicators. Data is used to guide decisions and interventions at the facility and community level.

dataElements.name:
drought-hw-car-1.3.5 Is the Health Workforce following guidance on risk assessments to assist in the identification, planning, monitoring 
 and evaluation of risk reduction and adaptation strategies associated with direct and in
dataElements.shortName: drought-hw-car-1.3.5 Is the Health Workforce follo
dataElements.description: 1: Not Provided:No community health education programme exists related to heatwave risks. No materials, sessions, or outreach efforts are in place. 2: Minimally Provided:Some informal or ad hoc efforts (e.g., verbal advice or posters) exist, but there is no structured programme or consistent outreach. Limited reach and low impact. 3: Basically Provided:A basic community health education programme is in place, reaching some sections of the population. It may include sporadic sessions, materials, or campaigns, but lacks wide coverage or regularity. 4: Moderately Providded:A structured and ongoing educational programme is available and implemented in most areas. It includes regular sessions, materials, and guidance focused on community resilience to heatwave risks. 5: Fully/Comprehensively provided:A comprehensive, well-funded, and community-wide health education programme is actively implemented. It effectively informs and empowers the public on preventive measures, symptoms of heat-related illnesses, and available services during heatwaves, with continuous monitoring and feedback loops to improve effectiveness.

dataElements.name: drought-hw-car-1.3.6 regularly participating in community disaster planning committees to: improve knowledge  on risk reduction, preparedness and respond to direct and indirect impacts of  drought hazard through adaptation measu
dataElements.shortName: drought-hw-car-1.3.6 regularly participating in co
dataElements.description: 1: No Participation:There is no representation of the health workforce in community-level disaster planning activities. Health input is missing from local adaptation strategies. 2: Minimal messaging:The need for public guidance during droughts has been recognised, but communication materials or training are yet to be developed. Messaging occurs on an ad-hoc basis. 3: Basic messaging:Draft messages and guidelines exist, highlighting food and water safety during droughts, but they have not been widely shared or tested. Some staff are aware of them but not all. 4: Moderate messaging:Preparedness messages on drought-related food and water safety are circulated among staff and sometimes shared with the community, but updates and consistent application are lacking. 5: Full messaging:There is a clear and regularly updated communication strategy that educates staff and community members on water and food safety before, during, and after droughts. Messaging is accessible, practical, and reinforced with community outreach.

dataElements.name:
drought-hw-car-1.3.7 Is the Health Workforce following an educational strategy to improve knowledge in the community on the social and 
 economic aspects of drought impacts, and how to reduce health risks and impacts?
dataElements.shortName: drought-hw-car-1.3.7 Is the Health Workforce follo
dataElements.description: 1: No educational strategy  in Place:There is no educational strategy or outreach targeting drought’s social and health-related effects in the community. Public awareness remains low and reactive. 2: Minimal educational strategy  in Place:Health workers have discussed the importance of community education around drought, but no strategy or programme is in place. A few informal sessions may have occurred. 3: Basic educational strategy:An educational plan has been drafted and piloted in select areas. Some staff have begun engaging communities, although reach and consistency are limited. 4: Moderate educaitonal strategy in Place:Community outreach on the health impacts of drought is ongoing, guided by an existing education plan. The strategy is effective but not regularly evaluated or 5: Full educatin strategy  in Place:Health workers actively educate communities using a well-developed and regularly updated strategy that addresses both the social and economic impacts of drought. Materials are locally appropriate and community engagement is strong.

dataElements.name: drought-hw-car-1.3.8 Is the Health Workforce provided with an effective emergency risk communication plan?*
dataElements.shortName: drought-hw-car-1.3.8 Is the Health Workforce provi
dataElements.description: 1: No Plan in Place:There is no structured emergency risk communication plan available to staff during drought events. Messaging is often reactive and inconsistent. 2: Minimal Plan:The need for an emergency communication plan has been recognised, and early-stage discussions have taken place. A draft may exist but is not yet implemented. 3: Basic Plan Drafted:A communication plan has been drafted, identifying key messages and communication channels. Training is limited and implementation is in progress. 4: Moderate Plan in Place:An emergency risk communication plan is being used, and staff are generally aware of their roles. The plan may lack updates to reflect evolving drought-related risks. 5: Fully Updated and Operational Plan:A comprehensive and regularly reviewed emergency communication plan is in place. Staff are trained to communicate clearly with communities and stakeholders during drought events.

dataElements.name: drought-hw-car-1.3.9 Is the Health Workforce Aware of keeping the facility cool by managing windows, curtains, lights, and using fans or cooler rooms to reduce heat exposure, especially when temperatures are below 35°C.
dataElements.shortName: drought-hw-car-1.3.9 Is the Health Workforce Aware
dataElements.description: 1: No awareness:There is no guidance on managing indoor temperatures during high heat or drought periods. Staff and patients may be exposed to unnecessary heat risks. 2: Minimal awareness:Cooling practices have been mentioned informally, but no procedures have been established. Knowledge is passed anecdotally without facility-wide application. 3: Basic awareness:Guidelines on keeping indoor spaces cool have been drafted and shared with some departments. Awareness is increasing, but practices are not consistent. 4: Moderate awareness:Cooling strategies are implemented in several parts of the facility, and staff follow basic procedures. However, there is no system for monitoring effectiveness or updating recommendations. 5: Full awareness:The facility maintains clear and up-to-date procedures to ensure indoor environments remain cool during heatwaves or droughts. Staff apply energy-efficient, practical measures daily and share this knowledge with patients.

dataElements.name:
drought-wash-car-2.1.1.Does the health care facility verify water safety conditions, which include updated risk assessments to map water
 resources and water supplies for the facility?
dataElements.shortName: drought-wash-car-2.1.1.Does the health care facili
dataElements.description: 1: No Verification in Place:The facility does not verify water safety conditions at all; no risk assessments or mapping of water resources have been conducted. 2: Minimal verification exists:The facility has begun some efforts but lacks formal or consistent verification; water safety checks or risk assessments are sporadic or outdated. 3: Basic verification done The facility conducts basic water safety verification, such as occasional risk assessments or informal mapping, but lacks a structured or regularly updated approach.: 4: Moderate Verification system in Place:The facility verifies water safety conditions regularly and has conducted some formal risk assessments and water resource mapping, though updates may not be frequent or fully comprehensive. 5: Fully Updated system:The facility has a comprehensive and routinely updated system for verifying water safety, including formal risk assessments and detailed mapping of all water resources and supplies.

dataElements.name:
drought-wash-car-2.1.2 Does the health care facility have an updated plan to map risks to the water and sanitation infrastructure to identify where
 services could be disrupted from water scarcity?
dataElements.shortName: drought-wash-car-2.1.2 Does the health care facili
dataElements.description: 1: No Risk mapping plan or awareness:The facility has no risk mapping plan or awareness regarding potential disruptions to water and sanitation infrastructure due to water scarcity. 2: Minimal awareness:There is limited awareness of water-related risks, but no documented plan or only informal practices are in place to address potential service disruptions. 3: Basic plan exists A risk mapping plan exists but is outdated or lacks detailed analysis; the plan may not cover all critical points of infrastructure vulnerability or water scarcity scenarios.: 4: Moderate plan in use:The facility has a reasonably current and partially comprehensive plan to identify risks; however, some key areas or scenarios may still be under-addressed. 5: Full/ Comprehensive plan is available:The facility has a regularly updated, detailed, and comprehensive risk mapping plan that clearly identifies vulnerabilities in water and sanitation infrastructure and outlines proactive strategies to mitigate disruptions from water scarcity.

dataElements.name: drought-wash-car-2.1.3 Does the health care facility regularly inspect the rainwater harvesting system for damage and contamination?
dataElements.shortName: drought-wash-car-2.1.3 Does the health care facili
dataElements.description: 1: No Inspection Conducted:The facility does not inspect the rainwater harvesting system at all, regardless of potential damage or contamination risks. 2: Minimal Inconsistent Inspection:Inspections are infrequent and only conducted in response to visible issues or complaints, not as part of a routine maintenance plan. 3: Basic inspection Exist:There is some effort to inspect the system, but checks are irregular and documentation is either missing or incomplete. 4: Moderate Regular Inspections Done:The system is inspected routinely, though not all components may be thoroughly checked, and some contamination risks may be overlooked. 5: Full/Compehensive Inspection exist:The facility has a structured and well-documented inspection schedule, with thorough checks for both damage and contamination conducted consistently.

dataElements.name: drought-wash-car-2.1.4 Does the health care facility have an evaluation system to monitor water drips, leaks and unnecessary flows in bathrooms, laundry facilities, kitchen, etc.; and perform prompt repairs to avoid loss?
dataElements.shortName: drought-wash-car-2.1.4 Does the health care facili
dataElements.description: 1: No Evaluation system:The health facility has no evaluation system or process to monitor water drips, leaks, or unnecessary flows. Issues are typically addressed only when they cause significant disruption. 2: Minimal Evaluation Checks:There are occasional, informal checks for water issues, but there is no structured system or schedule for monitoring. Repairs are reactive rather than planned or prompt. 3: Basic Evaluation Syatem:A basic evaluation system is in place to monitor water usage and identify leaks, but it may not cover all key areas (e.g., bathrooms, kitchen, laundry), and repairs are often delayed or inconsistently carried out. 4: Moderate Evaluation System:The facility has a working system to regularly monitor and detect water issues in major areas. Prompt repairs are made in many cases, but not all; resource or personnel limitations may affect response time. 5: Full/Comprehensive Evaluation System:There is a well-established and consistently implemented system for monitoring water drips, leaks, and unnecessary flows across all relevant areas. Issues are quickly identified and repaired, minimizing water loss and aligning with best practices in facility management.

dataElements.name: drought-wash-car-2.1.5  Does the health care facility verify safety conditions and proper functioning of all elements of the water distribution system in preparation for drought (e.g. storage tanks, cisterns, valves, pipes and c
dataElements.shortName: drought-wash-car-2.1.5  Does the health care facil
dataElements.description: 1: No Verification Process in Place:The facility does not conduct any checks or assessments on the water distribution system elements, even during periods of increased drought risk. 2: Minimal Process:The facility occasionally performs visual inspections or informal checks, but lacks a structured or consistent approach to verifying the system’s safety and functionality. 3: Basic Verification done:The facility conducts basic assessments of a few key components (e.g., storage tanks or visible pipes), but does not comprehensively verify all critical elements or conduct thorough safety evaluations. 4: Moderate Verification Active:There is a routine in place to verify most components of the water system, but some elements may be overlooked or inspections are not conducted with full technical rigor, especially in preparation for drought conditions. 5: Full Verification system:The facility has a well-established protocol for thoroughly inspecting all elements of the water distribution system, ensuring safety and full functionality, especially in preparation for drought. This includes regular documentation, maintenance, and timely interventions.

dataElements.name: drought-wash-car-2.1.6 Does the health care facility have information on the water system installation that ensures lower risk of being contaminated?
dataElements.shortName: drought-wash-car-2.1.6 Does the health care facili
dataElements.description: 1: No Information:The health facility has no information, documentation, or staff knowledge regarding the water system installation or its safety standards. 2: Minimal Information available:There is some verbal or informal understanding among staff, but there is no structured or accessible documentation on the water system installation or how it reduces contamination risk. 3: Basic undetailed information:The facility has basic information or partial documentation about the water system, but it does not clearly explain how contamination risks are managed or minimized. 4: Moderate Information exists:There is moderate-level information available, including documented installation details and general water safety measures, though some specifics may be missing. 5: Full/Comprehensive Information Developed:The facility has complete, up-to-date documentation on the water system installation, clearly detailing how it is designed and maintained to minimize contamination risk.

dataElements.name: drought-wash-car-2.1.7 Does the health care facility have a water quality monitoring plan for human consumption?
dataElements.shortName: drought-wash-car-2.1.7 Does the health care facili
dataElements.description: 1: No Monitoring Plan:The facility does not have any form of water quality monitoring plan in place for human consumption. 2: Minimal plan available:There are occasional or ad-hoc efforts to check water quality, but no formal or documented plan exists. 3: Basic Plan exists:A basic water quality monitoring plan exists, but it may not be comprehensive, regularly implemented, or consistently documented. 4: Moderate Plan Active:A structured and documented plan is in place and implemented periodically, but it may lack some resources, consistency, or complete coverage. 5: Full/Comprehensive plan Developed:The facility has a fully developed, documented, and consistently implemented water quality monitoring plan aligned with health standards and guidelines.

dataElements.name: drought-wash-car-2.1.8 Does the health care facility have a monitoring plan for potable water?
dataElements.shortName: drought-wash-car-2.1.8 Does the health care facili
dataElements.description: 1: No Plan exists:The facility has no documented or informal plan in place to monitor the quality or availability of potable water. 2: Minimal Plan:There is no formal plan, but the facility carries out occasional, undocumented checks or relies on visual assessments without consistency. 3: Basic Plan Drafted:A written plan exists, but it lacks detail, is not regularly implemented, or only covers limited aspects of potable water monitoring. 4: Moderate Plan in Place:The facility has a reasonably structured and partially implemented monitoring plan that covers key elements but may lack regular updates, full staff involvement, or complete documentation. 5: Fully Implemented Plan:The facility has a well-documented, regularly updated monitoring plan for potable water. The plan is actively implemented, involves responsible personnel, and is supported by routine reporting and corrective action procedures.

dataElements.name: drought-wash-car-2.2.1. Does the health care facility have a water management plan to identify water contamination?
dataElements.shortName: drought-wash-car-2.2.1. Does the health care facil
dataElements.description: 1: No Plan in Place:The facility does not have any water management strategy or framework to identify contamination. 2: Minimal Plan:There are early-stage discussions or considerations but no documented or active plan. 3: Basic Plan Drafted:a draft water management plan is available, focusing on identifying contamination but lacking implementation or regular review. 4: Moderate Plan in Operational:The plan is operational and includes protocols to identify contamination risks, though it may not be comprehensive or updated regularly. 5: Fully Implemented Plan:A complete, regularly reviewed, and operational water management plan is in place to effectively identify and respond to contamination.

dataElements.name: drought-wash-car-2.2.2. Does the health care facility have a contingency plan for monitoring and reducing contaminant concentrations in the facility water system supplies?
dataElements.shortName: drought-wash-car-2.2.2. Does the health care facil
dataElements.description: 1: No Contigency Plan Available:There is no documented or implemented plan for monitoring or addressing water system contamination. Staff are unaware of such protocols, and no efforts have been made to develop one. 2: Minimal Measures in Place:Some informal or ad-hoc measures are occasionally taken, but there is no formal, written contingency plan. These practices are inconsistent, lack documentation, and are not aligned with standard guidelines. 3: Basic Contigency Plan Exists:A basic contingency plan exists, possibly as part of a broader water safety or infection control plan. However, it is either outdated, lacks specific procedures for contaminant response, or is poorly implemented. 4: Moderate Plan is in Place:A functional contingency plan is in place with defined procedures for detecting and reducing contaminants. The facility conducts periodic monitoring, and staff are somewhat familiar with their roles during an incident. 5: Fully Functional and Implemented:A comprehensive, up-to-date contingency plan is in place and routinely implemented. The facility regularly monitors water quality, conducts training for staff, and promptly acts to mitigate any contamination. The plan aligns with national/international guidelines and is reviewed regularly.

dataElements.name: drought-wash-car-2.2.3 Does the health care facility have a water management system to avoid or reduce vector breeding sites?
dataElements.shortName: drought-wash-car-2.2.3 Does the health care facili
dataElements.description: 1: No management System Provided:The facility has no visible or documented water management system. Stagnant water is present, with no measures to prevent vector breeding. 2: Minimal Management Provided:There are isolated or informal attempts to manage water, but they are inadequate or poorly maintained, leading to persistent breeding sites. 3: Basic Mangement Approach Established:The facility shows evidence of rudimentary water management, such as occasional drainage or covering of water containers, but lacks a structured or consistent approach. 4: Moderate Management Available:A clear water management system exists and is implemented to a reasonable extent. While effective in some areas, gaps remain in coverage, maintenance, or staff training. 5: Fully Sufficient and Regular Management:The facility has a fully established and maintained water management system, regularly monitors and mitigates potential breeding sites, and aligns with best practices for vector control.

dataElements.name: drought-wash-car-2.2.4.Does the health care facility have anti-mosquito breeding measures to avoid vectorborne diseases?
dataElements.shortName: drought-wash-car-2.2.4.Does the health care facili
dataElements.description: 1: No Measures in Place:The facility has no visible or documented strategies to prevent mosquito breeding. There is stagnant water around, no drainage maintenance, and no vector control practices observed. 2: Minimal Anti-breeding Efforts:Some isolated efforts exist, such as occasional fumigation or irregular clearing of surroundings, but these are infrequent and not part of a structured plan. 3: Basic Measures Applied:The facility implements standard preventive steps like covering water containers and basic sanitation. However, efforts are limited in scope and not systematically monitored. 4: Moderate Measures in Place:The facility has a routine vector control program (e.g., regular fumigation, proper drainage), but some areas may still have breeding risks due to limited coverage or partial implementation. 5: Fully Implemented System:The facility has an active, well-implemented anti-mosquito strategy including regular monitoring, environmental management, community awareness, and zero tolerance for breeding grounds.

dataElements.name: drought-wash-car-2.2.5. Does the health care facility have a rainwater catchment system with safe water storage?
dataElements.shortName: drought-wash-car-2.2.5. Does the health care facil
dataElements.description: 1: No Catchment:The health facility has no rainwater catchment system or safe water storage mechanism in place. 2: Minimal Functional Catchment:There is evidence of a rainwater catchment system, but it is non-functional, poorly maintained, or lacks proper storage facilities to ensure water safety. 3: Basic Catchment in Use:A basic rainwater catchment system and storage exists, but it only functions occasionally, has limited capacity, or poses potential risks to water safety. 4: Moderate Catchment System:The facility has a functional rainwater catchment and safe storage system that meets part of its water needs but may have minor issues such as inconsistent maintenance or limited capacity. 5: Fully Operational Catchment System:The facility has a fully operational rainwater catchment system with well-maintained, safe water storage that consistently supports facility needs and aligns with safety standards.

dataElements.name: drought-wash-car-2.2.6. have water storage tanks with appropriate covers to prevent contamination?
dataElements.shortName: drought-wash-car-2.2.6. have water storage tanks w
dataElements.description: 1: No Storage in Place:The facility does not have any water storage tanks on site, and water is sourced directly from an unreliable or unsafe source. 2: Minimal Storage Available:There are water storage tanks available, but they are either in poor condition, lack proper covers, or are exposed in ways that risk contamination. 3: Basic Storage Compliants:The facility has water storage tanks with some form of cover, but not all covers are appropriate or in good condition to fully prevent contamination. 4: Moderate Storage System In Use:The majority of water storage tanks are in good condition with appropriate covers, reducing the risk of contamination, although minor improvements are needed. 5: Fully Equipped Storage Systems:All water storage tanks are present, well-maintained, and equipped with appropriate, secure covers that fully protect the stored water from contamination.

dataElements.name: drought-wash-car-2.2.7.Does the health care facility have water storage that is protected from direct sunlight?
dataElements.shortName: drought-wash-car-2.2.7.Does the health care facili
dataElements.description: 1: No Protected Storage Installed:The facility has no water storage system, or the existing storage is completely exposed to direct sunlight without any form of shading or protective covering. 2: Minimal Protection Exists:The water storage exists but has very minimal protection—such as a thin cloth or partial shading—which is ineffective against prolonged sun exposure. 3: Basic Protected Storage in Use:The water storage has a basic form of protection like a roof or a wall providing partial shade, but it does not consistently shield the tank from direct sunlight throughout the day. 4: Moderate Protection Available:The water storage is generally well protected, such as being placed in a shaded area or under a roof structure that significantly reduces sun exposure, though there might be brief periods where sunlight still reaches it. 5: Fully Covered and Sunlight-Protected:The water storage is entirely shielded from direct sunlight at all times, either by being placed indoors, underground, or under a fully covered structure that ensures consistent temperature control and sun protection.

dataElements.name: drought-wash-car-2.2.8.Does the health care facility have chemicals stored away from excessive heat?
dataElements.shortName: drought-wash-car-2.2.8.Does the health care facili
dataElements.description: 1: No Storage Protocol:Chemicals are stored in areas exposed to direct sunlight or near heat sources, posing serious safety and stability risks. 2: Minimal Separation from Risk:Some attempt has been made to avoid heat exposure, but the storage area still experiences high temperatures or lacks proper ventilation. 3: Basic Storage Measures:Chemicals are stored away from obvious heat sources, but the storage environment may still lack temperature regulation or consistent monitoring. 4: Moderate Anti-heat Storage Provided:Chemicals are stored in a reasonably cool and shaded area with some form of temperature control or passive heat protection. 5: Fully Protected from Heat:Chemicals are stored in a well-ventilated, temperature-controlled environment that ensures consistent protection from excessive heat, in line with best practices and safety standards.

dataElements.name: drought-wash-car-2.2.9.Does the health care facility provide sufficient drinking water to staff, patients and visitors?
dataElements.shortName: drought-wash-car-2.2.9.Does the health care facili
dataElements.description: 1: No Provision Available:The facility does not provide drinking water to staff, patients, or visitors at all. 2: Minimal Provision in Place:Drinking water is provided, but availability is inconsistent, limited to certain areas or times, and not accessible to all groups. 3: Basic Supply Coverage:Drinking water is available but may not be adequate in quantity or quality for all users (staff, patients, and visitors). 4: Moderate Provision Capacity:Drinking water is generally available and accessible in most areas, though occasional shortages or limited access may occur. 5: Full Provision Available:Drinking water is consistently available, clean, and easily accessible to all staff, patients, and visitors at all times.

dataElements.name: drought-wash-car-2.2.10 Does the health care facility have onsite water purification equipment to provide safe drinking water?
dataElements.shortName: drought-wash-car-2.2.10 Does the health care facil
dataElements.description: 1: No Equipment:The facility has no onsite water purification equipment and relies entirely on external or untreated sources for drinking water. 2: Minimal Functional Equipment:The facility has outdated or poorly maintained purification equipment that is rarely used or only partially functional. 3: Basic Purification Equipment in use:The facility has basic purification equipment in place, but its use is inconsistent or not fully integrated into daily water supply practices. 4: Moderate Equipment Usage:The facility has functional water purification equipment that is used regularly, though it may not serve the entire facility or meet peak demand. 5: Fully Operational Equipment:The facility has fully operational, well-maintained purification equipment that consistently provides safe drinking water to staff, patients, and visitors.

dataElements.name: drought-wash-car-2.2.11 Does the health care facility have a surveillance system for diseases related to water quality and sanitation?
dataElements.shortName: drought-wash-car-2.2.11 Does the health care facil
dataElements.description: 1: No Surveillance System:The health facility has no surveillance mechanism for tracking diseases related to water quality and sanitation. There is no formal or informal structure to detect or report such cases. 2: Minimal Surveillance Efforts:There are occasional or ad-hoc efforts to monitor water-related diseases, but no structured system or standard procedures exist. Reporting is inconsistent and not systematically documented. 3: Basic Surveillance in Place:A basic surveillance system is in place, possibly relying on manual reporting or basic health worker observations. However, it lacks standard protocols, regular updates, or integration with broader public health systems. 4: Moderate Surveillance System Active:The health facility maintains a functioning surveillance system with defined procedures for tracking water and sanitation-related diseases. 5: Fully Operational Surveillance System:The facility has a robust, systematic, and fully operational surveillance system that actively monitors, documents, and reports water and sanitation-related diseases.

dataElements.name: drought-wash-hsr-2.3.1. Does the health care facility have a long-term drought management plan, including the identification of available alternative safe water sources?
dataElements.shortName: drought-wash-hsr-2.3.1. Does the health care facil
dataElements.description: 1: No Drought Management Plan:There is no drought management plan in place, and no identification of alternative safe water sources has been considered. 2: Minimal Plan:The health facility has a minimal drought management plan, but alternative safe water sources have not been properly identified or addressed. 3: Basic Plan:The health facility has a basic drought management plan, and there is some consideration for alternative safe water sources, but the plan is not comprehensive or fully developed. 4: Moderate enagement:The health facility has a moderate drought management plan that includes identification of some alternative safe water sources, though further refinement and implementation may be needed. 5: Full Plan in Use:The health facility has a comprehensive, long-term drought management plan that thoroughly identifies and ensures the availability of alternative safe water sources.

dataElements.name: drought-wash-hsr-2.3.2.Does the health care facility have established procedures for procuring, transporting and safely storing water?
dataElements.shortName: drought-wash-hsr-2.3.2.Does the health care facili
dataElements.description: 1: No Procedures in Place:The facility does not have any documented or informal procedures for procuring, transporting, or storing water. These processes are either improvised or entirely absent. 2: Minimal Procedures:Some informal or ad-hoc methods exist, but they are not standardized, documented, or consistently followed. 3: Basic Procedures Exists:The facility has some established procedures for at least one aspect (e.g., procurement), but the other components (transportation and/or storage) are underdeveloped or lack proper implementation. 4: Moderate Established Procedures:There are documented and generally followed procedures for procuring, transporting, and storing water, but a few areas may still require improvement, such as regular reviews, training, or compliance monitoring. 5: Fully Documented Procedures:The facility has clear, comprehensive, and well-documented procedures covering procurement, transportation, and safe storage of water

dataElements.name: drought-wash-hsr-2.3.3.Does the health care facility work with water utility agencies to prevent suspension of services?
dataElements.shortName: drought-wash-hsr-2.3.3.Does the health care facili
dataElements.description: 1: No Engagement withb Agencies:The health facility does not engage or communicate with water utility agencies regarding service provision, and there are no known efforts to prevent service interruptions. 2: Minimal Engagement:There is little to no proactive interaction as the facility only reaches out to water utility agencies during emergencies or after services have already been disrupted. 3: Basic  Engagement:The facility has some level of communication with water utility agencies, possibly through occasional meetings or correspondence, but efforts to prevent suspension of services are not structured or consistent. 4: Moderate Engagement Adopted:The health facility maintains a working relationship with water utility agencies and takes some preventive steps, such as periodic check-ins or joint planning, though there may still be gaps in collaboration or response. 5: Full Engagement or collaboration:The facility has a well-established and proactive partnership with water utility agencies, including formal agreements or protocols to ensure continuity of water services and jointly manage risks of suspension.

dataElements.name: drought-wash-hsr-2.3.4.Does the health care facility have a water safety plan in place, in case of water contamination?
dataElements.shortName: drought-wash-hsr-2.3.4.Does the health care facili
dataElements.description: 1: No Safety Plan:The facility has no documented or informal plan to manage water contamination. Staff are unaware of any procedures or protocols for such an incident. 2: Minimal Plan:There may be verbal or informal discussions among staff regarding water safety, but no formal or written plan exists. There’s limited awareness or preparation. 3: Basic Plan Developed:The facility has a basic water safety plan, but it is either outdated, not well-communicated to all staff, or lacks comprehensive steps to respond to contamination. 4: Moderate Plan in Operation:A reasonably detailed water safety plan is available, known to relevant staff, and outlines key actions. However, drills or updates to the plan are infrequent. 5: Fully Implemented Plan   The facility has a detailed and up-to-date water safety plan that is well-disseminated, regularly reviewed, and practiced through routine training or simulations.:

dataElements.name: drought-wash-hsr-2.3.5.Does the health care facility have a plan to conserve and manage water to reduce water usage, specifically in case of prolonged drought?
dataElements.shortName: drought-wash-hsr-2.3.5.Does the health care facili
dataElements.description: 1: No Conservation Plan:The facility has no documented or verbal strategies in place to manage or conserve water during droughts or water shortages. 2: Minimal Plan:There are limited, informal practices (e.g., individual staff efforts) to reduce water usage, but no structured or facility-wide plan exists. 3: Basic Plan Demonstrated:The facility demonstrates some awareness of the need for water conservation, with a few basic measures in place (e.g., fixing leaks, water-saving signs), but lacks a comprehensive or formal plan. 4: Moderate Plan in Action:The facility has a documented plan or structured set of practices to conserve and manage water, with partial implementation and staff awareness, especially in preparation for drought scenarios. 5: Fully Documented and Updated Plan:The facility has a well-documented, regularly updated water conservation and management plan that is fully implemented, with clear roles, staff training, monitoring systems, and specific drought response strategies.

dataElements.name: drought-wash-hsr-2.3.6.Does the health care facility have a cross-sectoral water management plan to conserve and protect local or alternative water sources?
dataElements.shortName: drought-wash-hsr-2.3.6.Does the health care facili
dataElements.description: 1: No Cross-Sectoral Management Plan:The facility does not have any documented or informal water management plan, nor does it engage with other sectors or stakeholders regarding water conservation or protection. 2: Minimal Plan:There are minimal or ad-hoc efforts related to water management, possibly driven by individuals rather than formalized or coordinated plans. 3: Basic Plan Exists The facility has a basic water management plan that addresses some conservation measures, but it is not comprehensive or regularly updated: 4: Moderate Plan in Operation:The facility has a structured water management plan that includes clear conservation and protection strategies. 5: Fully Developed and Coordinated Plan:The facility has a fully developed and regularly reviewed water management plan that actively involves collaboration with multiple sectors (e.g., environment, public works, community groups)

dataElements.name: drought-wash-hsr-2.3.7.Does the health care facility Have a system to inspect alternative water sources (e.g. wells, dams) and, if needed, temporarily ban use until sanitary conditions are improved.
dataElements.shortName: drought-wash-hsr-2.3.7.Does the health care facili
dataElements.description: 1: No Mechanism in Place:There are no known policies, guidelines, or mechanisms in place for inspecting alternative water sources or enforcing restrictions based on sanitary conditions. 2: Minimal Mechanism Present:There is an informal or poorly defined process for water inspections, but it is rarely implemented and lacks the authority to enforce usage bans when needed. 3: Basic Mechanism Established:A basic policy or mechanism exists, and inspections are carried out occasionally. 4: Moderately Functional Mechanism:A formal mechanism is in place, inspections are conducted periodically, and there are documented procedures for issuing temporary bans, though these may not always be timely or fully enforced. 5: Fully Functional Mechanism:The facility has a well-established, routinely implemented mechanism for inspecting all alternative water sources, with a clear, enforceable process to temporarily ban use when sanitary risks are identified, aligning with public health safety standards.

dataElements.name: drought-wash-hsr-2.3.8.Does the health care facility have a contingency plan to ensure effective and timely delivery of safe water during drought and emergencies over the short-and long-term?
dataElements.shortName: drought-wash-hsr-2.3.8.Does the health care facili
dataElements.description: 1: No Contigency Plan Available:The facility has not developed any strategies or documentation to address water supply challenges during droughts or emergencies. 2: Minimal Plan Available:There may be some awareness or verbal strategies discussed, but there is no formal or documented contingency plan, and actions are reactive rather than planned. 3: Basic Contigency Plan Exists:A simple, written plan exists with limited scope, addressing only short-term or immediate needs, with minimal details on implementation or roles. 4: Moderate Plan Operational:The contingency plan covers both short-and long-term scenarios, includes some operational steps and assigned responsibilities, but may lack depth, regular updates, or testing. 5: Full/Comprehensive Plan Usage:A well-documented, regularly reviewed and tested plan exists

dataElements.name: drought-eng-maa-3.1.1 Does the health care facility regularly assess its energy system to ensure it can cope with drought conditions?
dataElements.shortName: drought-eng-maa-3.1.1 Does the health care facilit
dataElements.description: 1: No assessment:The facility does not evaluate its energy systems in relation to drought conditions. There is no awareness of how drought could impact power supply or energy demand. 2: Minimal Awareness:There have been limited conversations on the need for energy assessments during droughts, but no assessments have been performed. Staff are unclear about who is responsible. 3: Basic Assessment:A preliminary assessment framework has been drafted to evaluate the facility's energy resilience to drought. Some data collection has begun in key areas. 4: Moderate Assessment:The facility conducts energy assessments in relation to drought, though findings are not consistently used to inform upgrades or responses. Updates to systems or protocols are occasional. 5: Full/Comprehensive Assessment:Regular assessments are carried out to ensure energy systems can withstand drought conditions. Findings are used to strengthen resilience and guide proactive energy management.

dataElements.name: drought-eng-maa-3.1.2 Does the health care facility have an emergency backup generator (including fuel, where relevant) that is able to cover at least all critical service areas and equipment during and after the event?*
dataElements.shortName: drought-eng-maa-3.1.2 Does the health care facilit
dataElements.description: 1: No emergency backup:There is no backup generator or fuel supply to maintain operations during drought-induced power outages. The facility is at risk of service disruption. 2: Minimal emergency backup:The facility has identified the need for a generator, but one has not yet been installed. Discussions on capacity and coverage are underway. 3: Basic emergency backup:An emergency generator is available but may only partially support critical areas. There is uncertainty around runtime and fuel sufficiency. 4: Moderate emergency backup:A functioning generator is in place with adequate fuel for critical operations, but the plan lacks recent testing or capacity review. Coverage may not be optimal. 5: Fully functional emergency backup:A high-capacity generator with reliable fuel supply covers all critical areas during and after power interruptions. Regular tests and maintenance ensure full operational readiness.

dataElements.name: drought-eng-maa-3.1.3 Does the health care facility periodically check the emergency backup generator (including fuel, where relevant)?*
dataElements.shortName: drought-eng-maa-3.1.3 Does the health care facilit
dataElements.description: 1: No checks:The generator is not maintained or tested, and fuel levels are not routinely checked. It may not be functional in an emergency. 2: Minimal Checks:There is some awareness of the importance of testing the generator, but checks are rare and undocumented. Fuel monitoring is inconsistent. 3: Basic Checks:A basic maintenance schedule exists for checking the generator and fuel, with occasional implementation. Logs may be incomplete. 4: Moderate checks:Regular checks are conducted on the generator and fuel, but the frequency or thoroughness may need improvement. Some gaps exist in documentation. 5: Full checks:The generator and fuel are inspected and tested routinely according to a well-maintained schedule. All tests are logged, and performance is monitored.

dataElements.name: drought-eng-maa-3.1.4 Does the health care facility assess regularly heating, ventilation and air conditioning (HVAC) systems?
dataElements.shortName: drought-eng-maa-3.1.4 Does the health care facilit
dataElements.description: 1: No assessment:The facility does not evaluate its HVAC systems for efficiency or functionality, especially during drought or high-heat periods. Maintenance is reactive. 2: Minimal assessment:Some staff have flagged HVAC concerns, but no formal assessment has occurred. The equipment is only checked when issues arise. 3: Basic assessments:A plan exists to assess HVAC systems, and basic inspections have been performed. However, not all areas are included, and improvements are pending. 4: Moderate assessment:HVAC systems are evaluated periodically, and repairs or adjustments are made as needed. However, no recent upgrades or sustainability measures have been integrated. 5: Full Assessment:The facility conducts regular and thorough assessments of all HVAC systems. Maintenance ensures optimal performance and energy efficiency during drought conditions.

dataElements.name: drought-eng-maa-3.1.5 Does the health care facility assess whether renewable energy (if available, such as solar) is sufficient to power critical equipment?
dataElements.shortName: drought-eng-maa-3.1.5 Does the health care facilit
dataElements.description: 1: No Assessment of Renewable Energy:There is no consideration or evaluation of renewable energy capabilities. The facility relies entirely on traditional power sources. 2: Minimal Assessment:Initial discussions have begun about solar or other renewable sources, but assessments are informal. There is limited awareness of capacity needs. 3: Basic Assessment:A draft assessment has been conducted to gauge the effectiveness of renewable energy for critical systems. Data is limited but promising. 4: Moderate Assessment:Renewable systems have been evaluated and are used to support some critical services. Plans exist to expand capacity, but updates are needed. 5: Full assessments:Comprehensive assessments confirm renewable energy sources can support essential services. Systems are maintained and adjusted as energy needs evolve.

dataElements.name: drought-eng-rm-3.2.1. Does the health care facility have appliance thermometers in the refrigerator and freezer to determine if food, vaccines and other essential refrigeration-dependent medical supplies are safe?
dataElements.shortName: drought-eng-rm-3.2.1. Does the health care facilit
dataElements.description: 1: No Thermometers:The facility does not have thermometers installed in refrigeration units. Staff are unable to monitor safe temperatures for food and medical supplies. 2: Minimal thermometer awareness:There is limited awareness of the need for appliance thermometers, and some discussions have occurred. However, no thermometers have been procured or installed yet. 3: Basic thermometers:Thermometers have been installed in some critical refrigerators and freezers, but not consistently across all units. Monitoring procedures are basic and may not be recorded. 4: Moderate thermometers:Most critical refrigeration units are equipped with thermometers, and checks are performed periodically. However, procedures could be improved or updated. 5: Full thermomenters:All refrigeration units critical to health services are equipped with calibrated thermometers. Staff regularly record and monitor temperatures to ensure safety.

dataElements.name: drought-eng-rm-3.2.2.Does the health care facility have adequate daylight to ensure proper visibility during a power outage?
dataElements.shortName: drought-eng-rm-3.2.2.Does the health care facility
dataElements.description: 1: No daylight:No measures are in place to ensure sufficient natural lighting during power outages. Staff and patients may face visibility risks. 2: Minimal daylight:Some areas of the facility benefit from natural daylight, but no formal assessment has been conducted. Improvements have not yet been planned. 3: Basic daylight:An assessment of daylight adequacy has been drafted, and minor improvements have been suggested. Certain critical areas still require enhancement. 4: Moderate daylight:Most critical service areas have been assessed for daylight, and minor structural changes have improved natural lighting. However, a full review has not been recently conducted. 5: Full daylight:A comprehensive plan ensures critical service areas have adequate natural lighting during outages. Facility design considers daylight as part of emergency preparedness.

dataElements.name: drought-eng-rm-3.2.3. work with energy utility agencies to prevent suspension of electricity services
dataElements.shortName: drought-eng-rm-3.2.3. work with energy utility age
dataElements.description: 1: No Plan in Place:The facility has no coordination with utility providers to maintain power during critical periods. Risk of unplanned outages remains high. 2: Minimal Plan with Initial Discussions:Initial contacts with utility agencies have been made, but no formal agreements or plans are in place. Discussions are ongoing. 3: Basic Plan Drafted:A draft plan for coordinating with utilities exists and includes protocols for emergency support. Final agreements or MOUs are pending. 4: Moderate Plan in Place, Not Updated:The facility coordinates with utilities to some extent to prioritise services during drought or outages. However, the arrangements need to be reviewed and strengthened. 5: Fully Updated and Operational Plan:Strong partnerships with utility providers ensure electricity services are prioritised and sustained during emergencies. Agreements are reviewed and updated regularly.

dataElements.name: drought-eng-rm-3.2.4. Does the health care facility have power-operated doors that can be opened manually to permit exit in case of power failure?
dataElements.shortName: drought-eng-rm-3.2.4. Does the health care facilit
dataElements.description: 1: No Manual Option for Door:Power-operated doors are not equipped with manual override options. Egress may be compromised during a power outage. 2: Minimal Manual option:Some manual door-opening options have been identified, but comprehensive installation and staff training have not occurred. Risks remain in some areas. 3: Basic Manual Options:Plans to retrofit doors with manual overrides have been drafted, and work has begun. However, not all essential areas are covered yet. 4: Moderate Manual option:Most power-operated doors can be opened manually, but staff may not be regularly trained. Some minor upgrades may still be needed. 5: Full  Manual options:All power-operated doors have manual release mechanisms, and staff are trained on how to use them. Safety procedures are regularly tested.

dataElements.name: drought-eng-rm-3.2.5.Does the health care facility have a clear guidance on heat-risk management for the maintenance of critical infrastructure (e.g. air-conditioning, medical devices, computers, diagnostic equipment, boiling wa
dataElements.shortName: drought-eng-rm-3.2.5.Does the health care facility
dataElements.description: 1: No guidance:There is no guidance on managing heat risks for critical infrastructure during drought or high-heat periods. Equipment may fail without notice. 2: Minimal guidance:Staff recognise the risks of heat to critical equipment, but no formal guidance or maintenance strategy has been developed. Ad-hoc responses prevail. 3: Basic guidance:A draft heat-risk management guide exists and covers basic equipment like air-conditioning and computers. However, full implementation has not occurred. 4: Moderate guidance:Heat-risk protocols for infrastructure are in place and are generally followed, but they have not been updated recently. Some minor vulnerabilities remain. 5: Full guidance:The facility has clear, up-to-date heat-risk management guidance for all critical infrastructure. Staff are trained and systems are regularly maintained to withstand drought conditions.

dataElements.name: drought-eng-hsr-3.3.1.Does the health care facility have an emergency plan for power outages in the short-and long-term?
dataElements.shortName: drought-eng-hsr-3.3.1.Does the health care facilit
dataElements.description: 1: No Plan in Place:The facility does not have an emergency plan to address short-or long-term power outages. Staff respond reactively without structured procedures. 2: Minimal Plan:There have been initial discussions about the need for an emergency plan for power outages. However, no official plan has been developed or communicated to staff. 3: Basic Plan:A basic emergency power outage plan has been drafted, covering essential short-term actions. Long-term continuity measures are limited or yet to be detailed. 4: Moderate Plan:A functional emergency plan exists covering short-and long-term outages, but it has not been recently updated. Staff training and drills are occasional. 5: Full Plan:The facility has a robust, regularly updated emergency plan for managing both short-and long-term power outages. Staff are well-trained and drills are conducted routinely.

dataElements.name: drought-eng-hsr-3.3.2. Does the health care facility have a plan or regulation to determine ways to reduce overall energy use?
dataElements.shortName: drought-eng-hsr-3.3.2. Does the health care facili
dataElements.description: 1: No Plan:The facility has no policy or plan to reduce energy consumption. Energy-saving practices are not prioritised or monitored. 2: Minimal Plan:Some discussions on reducing energy use have taken place, but no formal plan or regulation is in place. Implementation of energy-saving measures is inconsistent. 3: Basic Plan:A draft plan exists outlining ways to reduce overall energy use in the facility. However, there is limited enforcement or monitoring. 4: Moderate Plan:The facility has a functioning energy reduction plan that staff generally follow. The plan requires updates to incorporate newer technologies or practices. 5: Full Plan:An effective, regularly updated energy reduction strategy is in place, promoting sustainable energy use. Staff are actively engaged in implementing energy-saving initiatives.

dataElements.name: drought-eng-hsr-3.3.3. Does the health care facility work with energy utility agencies to prevent suspension of electricity services?
dataElements.shortName: drought-eng-hsr-3.3.3. Does the health care facili
dataElements.description: 1: No Plan:There is no coordination with utility providers to safeguard electricity services during emergencies. Risk of service disruption remains high. 2: Minimal Plan:Initial contact with energy utilities has been made, but no concrete agreements have been secured. Coordination remains informal. 3: Basic Plan:A preliminary plan or agreement exists to maintain electricity services during emergencies, pending full implementation. Staff awareness of this arrangement is limited.. 4: Moderate Plan:The facility has agreements with utilities to prioritise electricity provision during crises, although they have not been revisited recently. Staff have basic understanding of protocols. 5: Full Plan:Strong and formal partnerships with utility providers ensure prioritised electricity services during droughts and emergencies. Agreements are reviewed regularly and fully integrated into emergency planning.

dataElements.name: drought-eng-hsr-3.3.4.Does the health care facility have an emergency plan to ensure availability of adequate lighting, communication and information systems, and refrigeration and sterilization equipment during a drought?*
dataElements.shortName: drought-eng-hsr-3.3.4.Does the health care facilit
dataElements.description: 1: No Plan:There is no emergency plan for maintaining lighting, communication, refrigeration, or sterilisation during drought conditions. Critical services may be severely disrupted. 2: Minimal Plan:Discussions have taken place about the importance of maintaining these critical systems, but an emergency plan has not been formalised. Preparations remain fragmented. 3: Basic Plan:A draft plan addresses maintaining essential systems like lighting, communication, refrigeration, and sterilisation during droughts. Some gaps in implementation remain. 4: Moderate Plan:The facility has an operational plan to ensure continuity of these critical services, although the plan could benefit from updates. Staff are somewhat familiar with the procedures. 5: Full plan:A comprehensive and regularly updated emergency plan ensures continuity of critical services during droughts. Staff are well-trained and systems are routinely tested.

dataElements.name:
drought-eng-hsr-3.3.5.Does the health care facility have a plan to evacuate patients to a cooling station if the facility has lost power and has no 
 other source of energy?
dataElements.shortName: drought-eng-hsr-3.3.5.Does the health care facilit
dataElements.description: 1: No Plan in Place:The facility does not have an evacuation plan to move patients to cooling stations during prolonged power loss. Vulnerable patients are at significant risk during heat events. 2: Minimal Plan with Initial Discussions:There is basic awareness about the need for evacuation to cooling centres, but no formal plan or procedures are in place. Staff may not know where or how to evacuate patients. 3: Basic Plan Drafted:A draft evacuation plan exists, identifying cooling stations and basic evacuation routes. Staff training on evacuation procedures is limited. 4: Moderate Plan:An evacuation plan is in place, including designated cooling stations and transportation logistics, but needs revision and regular drills. Some staff are familiar with their roles. 5: Full Plan:A fully developed, routinely updated evacuation plan ensures patients can be quickly moved to cooling stations. Staff are trained and evacuation drills are regularly conducted.

dataElements.name: drought-eng-hsr-3.3.6.Does the health care facility have a plan to ensure that the walls and roofs of the facility are insulated?
dataElements.shortName: drought-eng-hsr-3.3.6.Does the health care facilit
dataElements.description: 1: No Plan in Place:The facility has no plan or programme to ensure walls and roofs are insulated. Excessive heat gain during drought periods is unmanaged. 2: Minimal Plan with Initial Discussions:Some informal discussions have taken place regarding improving insulation, but no structured plan or actions have been adopted. Risks of heat exposure remain. 3: Basic Plan Drafted:A draft plan identifies insulation as a priority and outlines areas for improvement. Initial upgrades may have started on a small scale. 4: Moderate Plan in Place, Not Updated:Insulation upgrades have been implemented in several parts of the facility, based on an existing plan. A full facility-wide review is still pending. 5: Fully Updated and Operational Plan:A comprehensive plan ensures walls and roofs are properly insulated to improve thermal regulation. Regular assessments and maintenance activities are conducted.
